items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.q: What is the difference between strength and power?
items.correct: Strength is maximum force; power is force times speed of movement.
items.random_wrong: Strength is for the upper body, power for the lower.
items.why: Students conflate strength and power. A strong person can lift heavy slowly; a powerful athlete generates force QUICKLY (sprinter, shot-putter). Power requires both force and velocity.

items.correct: Information the performer senses from their own body during/after movement.
items.why: Students reverse the meaning. Intrinsic = from WITHIN (kinesthetic awareness, feel); extrinsic = from OUTSIDE (coach feedback, video, statistics). Experienced performers rely more on intrinsic feedback.

items.q: How many bones are in the adult human skeleton?
items.why: Correct answer: 206. “106” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Hip. “Elbow” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Number of bones in the adult human body?
items.why: Exact answer: 206 bones. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
